name: Peoples Temple Full
description: The Peoples Temple, officially the Peoples Temple Christian Church Disciples of Christ, was an American religious organization founded in 1955 by Jim Jones. Initially promoting racial equality and social justice, the Temple attracted a diverse following, particularly in Indiana and later in California. Jones gradually exerted increasing control over his followers, demanding absolute loyalty and implementing increasingly bizarre and manipulative practices. He preached a socialist ideology and promised a utopian existence, but his leadership evolved into a cult of personality. The Temple relocated to Guyana in the mid-1970s, establishing a settlement known as Jonestown. Concerns about human rights abuses and reports of brainwashing led to investigations. On November 18, 1978, over 900 people, including Jones himself, died in Jonestown in a mass suicide/murder orchestrated by Jones, primarily through cyanide-laced Flavor-Aid. The event shocked the world and remains one of the largest single losses of American civilian life in a non-natural disaster. Mainstream scholarship views the Peoples Temple as a dangerous cult led by a charismatic but deeply disturbed individual who exploited his followers' vulnerabilities and manipulated them into committing mass suicide. Psychological manipulation, social isolation, and fear tactics are identified as key factors in Jones's ability to control the Temple's members. Alternative interpretations, often found within conspiracy theory circles, suggest external forces were involved, potentially government agencies or other shadowy organizations, though these claims lack credible evidence and are generally dismissed by historians and researchers.
subtitle: Tragic cult and mass suicide event

claims.claim: Jim Jones was a divine prophet.
claims.evidence: Testimonies of followers who witnessed Jones's apparent healing abilities and prophecies.
claims.criticism: Skepticism regarding the validity of the claimed miracles and prophecies. Attributing them to deception, psychological manipulation, and staged events.

claims.claim: The mass suicide was an act of revolutionary suicide to protest societal ills.
claims.evidence: Jones's rhetoric about revolutionary suicide and escaping the evils of the outside world.
claims.criticism: The event was primarily a mass murder orchestrated by Jones, using coercion and manipulation to force his followers to consume poison. The 'revolutionary suicide' narrative was a justification for his actions.

claims.claim: External forces, such as the CIA, were involved in the Jonestown massacre.
claims.evidence: Circumstantial evidence and alleged inconsistencies in official reports.
claims.criticism: These claims lack credible evidence and are based on speculation and conjecture. No reliable evidence supports the involvement of external forces in the mass suicide/murder.

keyFigures.name: Jim Jones
keyFigures.role: Founder and leader of the Peoples Temple

keyFigures.name: Deborah Layton
keyFigures.role: Former Temple member who defected and exposed the cult's abuses

keyFigures.name: Tim Reiterman
keyFigures.role: Journalist and author of 'Raven: The Untold Story of Jim Jones and His People'

timeline.event: Jim Jones founds the Peoples Temple in Indianapolis, Indiana.
The Temple relocates to California and gains a large following.
The Temple begins construction of Jonestown in Guyana.
The Jonestown mass suicide/murder occurs, resulting in over 900 deaths.

sources.author: Reiterman, Tim
sources.work: Raven: The Untold Story of Jim Jones and His People
sources.title: Reiterman, Tim

sources.author: Layton, Deborah
sources.work: Seductive Poison: A Jonestown Survivor's Story of Faith and Deceit in the Peoples Temple
sources.title: Layton, Deborah

variants: Peoples Temple Christian Church Disciples of Christ
